parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I used to breeze through unexpected changes without a second thought, but lately even the smallest shift in my routine leaves me completely rattled. I catch myself holding my breath over things that never used to bother me at all. My chest gets tight and my thoughts start spinning out of control before I can even catch myself. It is just a weird feeling to watch my own reactions spiral over absolutely nothing. I keep waiting for it to pass, but the tension just lingers in the background.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you're going through a really challenging time right now. It's completely understandable to feel rattled when your usual way of handling change isn't working anymore. That feeling of your chest tightening and your thoughts spinning out of control can be incredibly unsettling, especially when it feels like it's happening over things that used to be no big deal.

It takes a lot of self-awareness to notice these shifts in your reactions and to articulate them so clearly. It's also tough when you're waiting for that tension to pass, only for it to linger.

Have you noticed anything specific that might have triggered this change in how you're reacting to unexpected shifts? Sometimes understanding the "why" can be a first step, even if it doesn't immediately solve the "how" to feel better.

turns.user_message: What really gets to me is how this reaction has become my new normal instead of just a passing phase. I used to just shake off that nervous energy, but now it builds up until I feel completely overwhelmed by my own heartbeat. Every little surprise feels like a warning sign that something is about to go wrong. I try to ground myself by taking deep breaths, but the panic just keeps creeping back in anyway. It is exhausting to constantly monitor my own body for signs of trouble.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly draining to be living with that constant sense of unease and to feel like your body is always on high alert. It's completely understandable that you're feeling exhausted when you're putting so much energy into managing that nervous energy and trying to ward off panic.

It's a really tough place to be when something that used to be manageable now feels overwhelming, and when everyday surprises trigger such a strong reaction. The effort you're putting into grounding yourself with deep breaths, even when it feels like the panic still creeps back, shows a lot of resilience. It's a testament to how much you're trying to cope with this.

Constantly monitoring your own body for signs of trouble must be so taxing. It's like having an internal alarm system that's always on, and it's natural to feel worn down by that.

turns.user_message: I honestly do not recognize myself anymore because this constant state of alert has completely taken over my everyday life. I used to laugh at people who got worked up over minor details, but now I am the one trapped in that exact cycle. My mind races so fast that I feel like I am about to jump out of my own skin. Every single moment feels heavy and urgent, leaving me completely drained before the day even really starts. I just want to go back to feeling steady and calm without having to fight for it.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you're going through an incredibly difficult time, and it's completely understandable that you're feeling this way. That constant state of alert, where your mind races and every moment feels heavy and urgent, is exhausting and can really make you feel disconnected from yourself. It's like your nervous system is stuck in overdrive, and it's taking a huge toll on your energy and your sense of peace.

It's also really insightful that you recognize how you've become the person you used to observe. That kind of self-awareness, even in the midst of distress, is a sign of your strength. It shows you're still connected to your past self and that you have a clear desire to return to that feeling of steadiness and calm.

The feeling of wanting to "jump out of your own skin" is a powerful description of how overwhelming this can be. It's like your body and mind are in a constant battle, and it's no wonder you feel drained before the day even begins.

Please know that you're not alone in experiencing this, and it's a valid and significant struggle. Wanting to go back to feeling steady and calm without having to fight for it is a very natural and important desire. It takes immense courage to articulate these feelings, and I want to acknowledge how brave you are for sharing them.
